_meta.purpose: Map the source's distinct ideas + central tension BEFORE writing any questions. Forces breadth — without this step, Claude concentrates on the single most quotable framework and misses the broader argument.

central_tension: Cat Wu argues against the traditional PM toolkit (multi-quarter roadmaps, partner-team alignment, detailed PRDs, hiring more PMs) and in favor of an AI-native PM mode: speed, product taste, end-to-end engineering ownership, and removing every barrier to shipping. The PM either adapts the role around iteration speed and taste, or becomes the bottleneck.

ideas.title: AGI-pilled calibration
ideas.summary: PMs should build for the model they have today, not the hypothetical future model. Eliciting maximum capability from current models is the harder, higher-leverage skill.
ideas.supporting_passage: I think it is very hard to be the right amount of AGI-pilled. It's very easy to build the product for the super AGI strong model. The hard thing is figuring out for the current model, how do you elicit the maximum capability?

ideas.title: Remove every barrier to shipping
ideas.summary: The operating principle isn't process optimization — it's barrier elimination. Six-month feature timelines compressed to one week (sometimes one day) by removing coordination overhead.
ideas.supporting_passage: We want to remove every single barrier to shipping things. The timelines for a lot of our product features have gone down from six month to one month and sometimes to even one day.

ideas.title: PM toolkit obsolescence
ideas.summary: The hard truth: many PMs are 'approaching it very incorrectly.' The traditional toolkit (multi-quarter roadmap alignment, detailed PRDs, partner coordination) was built for a slower world and is now the bottleneck, not the lever.
ideas.supporting_passage: You're interviewing hundreds of PMs and you just keep feeling like they're approaching it very incorrectly.

ideas.title: Product taste as the rare PM skill
ideas.summary: As code becomes cheap to write, deciding *what* to write becomes the differentiator. Product taste is rare, and Anthropic will hire anyone who has demonstrated it strongly.
ideas.supporting_passage: I think product taste is still a very rare skill to have, and we'll pretty much hire anyone who we feel has demonstrated this strongly.

ideas.title: Roles are merging
ideas.summary: PMs do engineering work; engineers do PM work; designers ship code. The clean role boundaries that defined the previous era of product development are collapsing.
ideas.supporting_passage: I think all of the roles are merging. PMs are doing some engineering work, engineers are doing PM work, designers are PMing and also landing code.

ideas.title: Concept corners
ideas.summary: Carve out a small product surface where one engineer (or PM) can take an idea from inception to user hands within a week — without multi-quarter roadmap alignment.
ideas.supporting_passage: as a PM, there should be less emphasis on making sure that you're aligning your multi-quarter roadmaps with your partner teams and more emphasis on, okay, how can we figure out the fastest way to get something out the door?

ideas.title: End-to-end engineer ownership
ideas.summary: On Anthropic's team, engineers go from user feedback on Twitter to a shipped product by week's end with almost no PM involvement. This is the most efficient way to ship.
ideas.supporting_passage: There are many engineers on our team who are fully able to end to end go from see user feedback on Twitter through to ship a product at the end of the week with almost no product involvement.

_notes_to_pass_1: Seven distinct ideas spanning four central + three secondary. Coverage requirement (Pass 1): generate 7 candidate questions distributed across these idea_ids; max 2 questions per idea; Pass 2 keeps 5 covering at least 4 distinct ideas.
Central tension is the through-line: every kept question should let a PM detect 'old toolkit vs AI-native PM' from the question framing alone.
